text:
There's no precise definition, and I doubt one could neatly distinguish between the two, but the idea is
• Theoretical language: formal language for use in theorems and proofs. Example: lambda calculus.
• Concrete language: formal language used for writing programs to be run on a computer.
It's easy to find hybrids and counterexamples (APL comes to mind), but overall the distinction looks useful to me.

The path from K&R C to ANSI C is an example used in the paper. As for Unix, Wikipedia agrees with Claude: it was written in PDP-7 assembler initially.

text: I’m curious if this could be implemented as a library, at least as a way to test out the ideas / API. If so, it seems strange to jump straight to a platform-level API.

text:
Just relistened to the 4 year old FoC episode on Man-Computer Symbiosis by J.C.R. Licklider: <https://feelingof.com/episodes/055/>
Sort of uncanny now. The skepticism made sense then, but post-LLMs it feels a lot more like Lickie was describing the thing starting to arrive now...

text:
This description leaves me unsatisfied. The implication is that Sketchpad, UNIX V1, TurboPascal V1, Forth, Lisp 1.5, etc., were programmed in 'informal languages'. Is that a 3rd category?

Yet, IMO, assembler is "more formal" than machine code. And machine code is "more formal" than a breadboard filled with zillions of transistors and a spaghetti network of wires.

If there is a 3rd category, what is the difference between "informal languages" and "concrete languages"?

There seems to be an underlying implication that "concrete languages" are "better" than the kind of languages used for Sketchpad, UNIX V1, TurboPascal 1, etc. How? Was Sutherland being ad hoc or was he being "formal" in a way that is no longer considered "formal" by modern standards?

A technique that was used in the transition from transistors to machine code was to lasso functionality into small chunks and to create something akin to strands of DNA in the form of machine code subroutines that can be walked and interpreted by little barnacle processors (akin to RNA?). Could we vault to expressing new levels of complexity by re-applying this technique?

There seems to be an underlying implication that "concrete languages" are "better" than the kind of languages used for Sketchpad, UNIX V1, TurboPascal 1, etc. How? Was Sutherland being ad hoc or was he being "formal" in a way that is no longer considered "formal" by modern standards?

A technique that was used in the transition from transistors to machine code was to lasso functionality into small chunks and to create something akin to strands of DNA in the form of machine code subroutines that can be walked and interpreted by little barnacle processors (akin to RNA?). text: [btw, IMO, K&R C and ANSI C are 2 completely different languages. The former accentuates building systems software for a PDP-11 by generating assembler (albeit using only PVRsubroutines (parameterized, value-returning subroutines)) and the latter emphasizes solving type-consistency riddles]

elements.text: [btw, IMO, K&R C and ANSI C are 2 completely different languages. The former accentuates building systems software for a PDP-11 by generating assembler (albeit using only PVRsubroutines (parameterized, value-returning subroutines)) and the latter emphasizes solving type-consistency riddles]

text: I've been pretty addicted to getting Claude to build the little standalone html/javascript artifacts lately and a few of my friends have caught the bug. I feel like there are a lot of possibilities with them - beyond just creating tons of Bret Victor-style explorable explanations (which is also a ton of fun). Wrote up a little piece about it and this idea around a social network for software that might become possible: <https://worksonmymachine.ai/p/artifacts-are-alive-and-photographs>

attachments.fallback: Artifacts Are Alive (And Photographs are Dead)
attachments.text: There is a photograph of a coral reef on the wall of my dentist’s office.
attachments.title: Artifacts Are Alive (And Photographs are Dead)

text: Also something else that might be interesting to some of you here - I'm playing around with this UX idea of "bring your own claude code" or "bring your own agent" - instead of me setting up any inference or AI on my site, it's designed so that you can point your claude code at it and build things rather than having to pay for a subscription and buy credits and all that nonsense... I gave a talk about it yesterday to another group but also happy to share more here if anyone is interested... the start of the idea is here: <https://artifact.land/cli>

attachments.fallback: artifact.land: aland — artifact.land from your terminal
attachments.text: Install aland, the artifact.land command-line client. Publish artifacts from your terminal, or let a coding agent publish on your behalf.
attachments.title: aland — artifact.land from your terminal
